title: Employee Hierarchy with Construction Order and Overrides
prompt_md:
## Goal

The `Person` base class below is given (do not modify it). Complete a three-level hierarchy `Person` -> `Employee` -> `Manager` that exercises the whole chapter at once: base-constructor delegation, construction/destruction order, added members, and redefining `describe()` to extend the base version via `Base::describe()`.

## Input

- Line 1: a string `name` (no spaces), an integer `age`, and a string `department` (no spaces).
- Line 2: an integer `teamSize`.

## Output

First the construction trace (in the order constructors actually run):

```
Person constructed: <name>, age <age>
Employee constructed: <department>
Manager constructed: team of <teamSize>
```

Then a blank line, then the manager's `describe()` result:

```
<name> (age <age>), <department> dept, manages <teamSize>
```

Then a blank line, then the destruction trace (reverse order):

```
Manager destroyed
Employee destroyed
Person destroyed
```

## Constraints

- `Employee` adds a department, delegates name/age to `Person`, prints `Employee constructed: <department>` in its constructor and `Employee destroyed` in its destructor, and redefines `describe()` to return `Person::describe()` followed by `, <department> dept`.
- `Manager` adds a team size, delegates name/age/department to `Employee`, prints `Manager constructed: team of <teamSize>` and `Manager destroyed`, and redefines `describe()` to return `Employee::describe()` followed by `, manages <teamSize>`.
- In `main`, build the `Manager` inside a scope block; between construction and destruction print a blank line, the `describe()` result, and another blank line. The construction and destruction order must come from the language, not from manual sequencing.

test_cases.label: Sample -- typical manager
test_cases.expected_stdout:
Person constructed: Alice, age 35
Employee constructed: Engineering
Manager constructed: team of 8

Alice (age 35), Engineering dept, manages 8

Manager destroyed
Employee destroyed
Person destroyed

test_cases.label: Hidden -- single-person team
test_cases.expected_stdout:
Person constructed: Bob, age 28
Employee constructed: Sales
Manager constructed: team of 1

Bob (age 28), Sales dept, manages 1

Manager destroyed
Employee destroyed
Person destroyed

test_cases.label: Hidden -- large team older manager
test_cases.expected_stdout:
Person constructed: Zara, age 60
Employee constructed: Research
Manager constructed: team of 50

Zara (age 60), Research dept, manages 50

Manager destroyed
Employee destroyed
Person destroyed

title: Protected Access and Redefining Inherited Functions
prompt_md:
## Goal

The `Vehicle` base class below is given (do not modify it). Write a `RaceCar` that publicly inherits from it and combines several chapter ideas: it reads `protected` members directly to add a `boost()` behavior, and it **redefines** the inherited `setSpeed()` so that a race car refuses arbitrary speed changes.

## Input

- Line 1: a string `model` (no spaces), an integer `topSpeed`, and an integer `currentSpeed`.
- Line 2: an integer `boostCount`.

## Output

First the initial status, then one line per boost, then the status again, then the disabled-setter message:

```
<model>: <currentSpeed>/<topSpeed> km/h
Boost 1: <speed> km/h
Boost 2: <speed> km/h
...
<model>: <finalSpeed>/<topSpeed> km/h
setSpeed is disabled for RaceCar
```

Each boost adds 30 km/h to the current speed but never exceeds `topSpeed`. The last line is produced by calling `setSpeed(0)` on the `RaceCar`, which must print the disabled message instead of changing the speed.

## Constraints

- `RaceCar` uses the inherited `protected` speed members directly to implement `boost()` (add 30, capped at `topSpeed`).
- `RaceCar` redefines `setSpeed(int)` so it prints `setSpeed is disabled for RaceCar` and leaves the speed unchanged.
- `main` builds a `RaceCar`, prints the status, applies `boostCount` boosts (printing each result as `Boost <i>: <speed> km/h`), prints the status again, then calls `setSpeed(0)`.

test_cases.label: Sample -- three boosts with cap
test_cases.expected_stdout:
Ferrari: 150/300 km/h
Boost 1: 180 km/h
Boost 2: 210 km/h
Boost 3: 240 km/h
Ferrari: 240/300 km/h
setSpeed is disabled for RaceCar

test_cases.label: Hidden -- boost hits top speed cap
test_cases.expected_stdout:
GoKart: 50/80 km/h
Boost 1: 80 km/h
Boost 2: 80 km/h
GoKart: 80/80 km/h
setSpeed is disabled for RaceCar

test_cases.label: Hidden -- zero boosts
test_cases.expected_stdout:
Sedan: 0/200 km/h
Sedan: 0/200 km/h
setSpeed is disabled for RaceCar
